title: Slow to Speak
reading:
The second instruction James gives is slow to speak. Once you have listened — really listened — what you do with your words next determines everything.

What it really means: take in what is being said rather than responding immediately. Choose your words carefully instead of using them as weapons or shields. Silence is allowed while you think.

Slow to speak means:
You can pause before you respond.
You can say "give me a minute" — and that is wisdom, not weakness.
It's not something you have to fix right away.

Proverbs 29:11 says a fool gives full vent to his spirit, but a wise man quietly holds it back. That's not suppression — it's stewardship. Choosing what you release, and when, and how.

The opposite of slow to speak shows up when the emotion takes over:
Exploding in rage.
Lying out of fear — saying anything to make the pressure stop.
Yelling to prove a point.
Saying whatever comes to mind just to end the conversation.

None of those produce righteousness. They produce more conflict, more hurt, and more distance. Words said in anger rarely represent what you actually believe — they represent what you feel in the worst moment of a hard conversation.

Slow to speak is not silence forever. It's choosing the right moment, with the right heart, to say the right thing.
reflection: Is there something you have said in anger that you wish you could take back? What does that tell you about the importance of being slow to speak?
abide: Lord, set a guard over my mouth. Help me hold back words that wound and choose words that heal.
